title: If You’re Searching for a New Skillet, Consider Stainless Steel
gist: A hands-on skillet test argues stainless steel is winning as Teflon was dropped over harmful-fume concerns and ceramic loses nonstick fast; challenger brands Heritage Steel and Hestan out-tested incumbent All-Clad, whose flagship D3 showed quality-control flaws.

insights.claim: The cookware industry largely abandoned the Teflon nonstick category due to its propensity to give off harmful fumes when overheated, and the ceramic that filled the void loses its nonstick performance quickly.
insights.speaker: Joe Ray
insights.so_what: A durable category shift away from coated nonstick toward stainless favors makers of clad stainless pans over legacy nonstick brands.
insights.quote: the industry pretty much dropped the whole Teflon category like a hot potato due to the pans’ propensity to give off harmful fumes if they get too hot
insights.locator: At the Mall of New Hampshire in the 1980s

insights.claim: Two All-Clad D3 fry pans sent for testing showed quality issues (a domed cooking surface within All-Clad's tolerance but warped to the tester, and improperly squished handle rivets on one), undercutting the brand's buy-it-for-life reputation.
insights.speaker: Joe Ray
insights.so_what: Visible QC lapses at a category-defining incumbent open share to challenger brands in premium cookware.
insights.quote: I learned that this amount of doming is within All-Clad’s tolerance range, but not within mine.

insights.claim: Heritage Steel's titanium-series pan handled prolonged high-heat stir-frying without warping and its 10.5-inch model costs $150, the same price as All-Clad's 10-inch D3, making the challenger a direct value threat to the incumbent.
insights.speaker: Joe Ray
insights.so_what: Price-parity plus better durability is how challenger cookware brands erode incumbent pricing power.
insights.quote: the 10.5-inch version of the Heritage pan is $150, the same price as All Clad's 10-inch D3 skillet
insights.locator: The giant 12-inch Titanium Series skillet from Heritage Steel

insights.claim: Hestan's new ProBond Luxe ($150-$200) differentiates on design features rivals lack: countersunk rivets for a smoother interior, curved sidewalls aiding the sauté snap, and sealed rims making it dishwasher-safe.
insights.speaker: Joe Ray
insights.so_what: Feature differentiation (dishwasher-safe sealed rims) is how a premium brand justifies pricing against commoditized clad stainless.
insights.quote: it's one of the few brands out there that embrace the dishwasher
insights.locator: three things about the Hestans’ design jumped out at me

insights.claim: Viking's new Pure Glide Pro, despite a promising textured-titanium nonstick layer, either warped or arrived warped and ran more than twice as hot as competitors (mid-to-high 300s F versus ~150 F), making it not yet recommendable.
insights.speaker: Joe Ray
insights.so_what: Execution failures on a promising design show that materials innovation alone does not win the category without build quality.
insights.quote: The two Vikings, though, were essentially giant hot spots, with the surfaces of each pan reaching the mid to high 300s, more than twice as hot as the competition.
